original_abstract: The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) was founded in 1863 and is often considered the gold standard in humanitarian action. Despite its many positive achievements over more than 150 years, some former ICRC officials believe that the organization is now in decline because of a series of recent policy choices. Their view is that the organization has undermined its reputation for independent and neutral humanitarian action, while growing too fast and too large, which has weakened its reputation for quick, tightly focused, and effective action in the field. David P. Forsythe revisits the ICRC policy decisions of recent decades and suggests that the organization is not in fatal decline, but that it does need to reconsider some of its policies at the margins. Though some errors have been made and some corrections are in order, Forsythe argues that its obituary is premature.
humanized_abstract: The ICRC was founded in 1863 and, arguably, is the leading agency in the world of humanitarian affairs. Over 150 plus years, it has done many wonderful things but a small group of former ICRC officials claim the organization is in a state of decline due to current policies which they claim have harmed the organization’s neutral and independent image as well as undermined its humanitarian competence. Specifically, they claim the organization has grown too rapidly and become too large. It is capable for achieving fewer real humanitarian goals more quickly and efficiently because of the way the organization has developed over the past several decades. In this Issue Brief, David P. Forsythe weighs the pros and cons of ICRC policies and practices over recent years and disagrees with the characterization of a humanitarian agency that has done a tremendous amount of good over time as being in “fatal decline.” There have undoubtedly been some poor staff decisions as well as a few policy mistakes over the years that should be corrected, but an obituary of this humanitarian institution as a whole is premature.
